title: Behavioral Emergency: Leading the Team Response to an Escalating Patient
subtitle: A 38-year-old on the unit is pacing, shouting, and threatening with clenched fists. The nurse must lead a coordinated team response — ensuring safety, de-escalating with one calm voice, and using restraint only as a last resort.

patient.name: Derek Olsen
patient.admitDate: Behavioral health unit. Rapidly escalating agitation — pacing, shouting, clenched fists, and verbal threats — posing an imminent risk to himself and others. Nurse leading the behavioral response team.

labs.r: Time-limited; RN may initiate in emergency.

nursesNote: Derek Olsen, 38, rapidly escalating (pacing, shouting, clenched fists, threats) with imminent risk. The nurse leads a coordinated team response: ensure safety first (maintain personal space and an exit, remove other patients and hazards, never confront alone), activate the behavioral response team early, and assign roles with closed-loop communication (one calm spokesperson, positioning/safety, security, medication nurse, timekeeper/documenter). Use the least-restrictive approach — verbal de-escalation and an offered oral PRN before physical measures. Restraint/seclusion is a LAST resort for imminent danger, requires a time-limited provider order (never PRN; RN may initiate in an emergency and obtain the order ASAP), and demands continuous monitoring with the earliest safe release. Provide post-event monitoring, a staff and patient debrief, and objective documentation.
providerOrders: Activate the behavioral response team; ensure staff and patient safety
Designate one leader/spokesperson; assign roles with closed-loop communication
Use least-restrictive measures first (verbal de-escalation, offered oral PRN)
Restraint/seclusion only for imminent danger with a time-limited order (never PRN); monitor continuously
Provide post-event monitoring, a staff/patient debrief, and objective documentation

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What does this escalating situation require?

opts.text: Early recognition of escalation allows a safer, planned response
Staff and bystander safety is a priority (personal space, exits, remove hazards)
Calling for help early and activating a behavioral response team is appropriate
One designated leader/spokesperson should communicate with the patient
A single nurse should confront an aggressive patient alone to save time
Restraint/seclusion is a last resort for imminent danger, not a first step
Closed-loop communication and clear role assignment keep the team coordinated
Verbal de-escalation and offering choices/oral PRN come before physical measures
Restraint/seclusion requires a provider order and is never a PRN/standing order
A restrained or secluded patient needs continuous monitoring and timely release
Multiple staff talking to the patient at once is the best approach
A post-event debrief with staff and patient supports safety and learning
Ensure safety: personal space/exit, remove others and hazards
Activate the behavioral response team and assign roles
Use one spokesperson for verbal de-escalation and offer oral PRN
If imminent danger persists, use least-restrictive restraint with an order
Monitor, debrief, and document after the event

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Does the action support a safe team response?

opts.text: Designate one leader/spokesperson to communicate calmly
Call the behavioral response team early and assign roles
Try verbal de-escalation and offer an oral PRN first
Remove other patients and hazards; keep an exit available
Use restraint only as a last resort for imminent danger, with an order
Continuously monitor and debrief after a restraint
Have several staff shout instructions at once
Confront the escalating patient alone in a corner
Apply restraints as a first-line or PRN measure

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Call for help and bring the team; keep yourself and others safe
Assign roles with closed-loop communication
Have one spokesperson de-escalate and offer choices/oral medication
Escalate to physical restraint only if needed, with an order and monitoring
Provide post-event monitoring, debrief, and documentation
One calm spokesperson de-escalates and the patient accepts oral medication
Roles are assigned with closed-loop communication and the team stays coordinated
Other patients are moved to safety and hazards are removed
After restraint, the patient is continuously monitored and the team debriefs
Staff crowd and shout at the patient, escalating the situation
The nurse confronts the patient alone without backup
Restraints are applied as punishment without less-restrictive measures
A restrained patient is left alone and unmonitored
